files.SKILL.md:
---
name: signal-and-verify
description: Generic task execution — observe before acting, write outputs explicitly, validate end-state not just configuration.
---

# SKILL — Signal and Verify

## What Counts as Finished
The verifier passes on the actual artifact. Not the reasoning. Not the intermediate steps. The thing the task asked for must exist, be reachable, and behave correctly when tested. Claiming done before running the verifier is a guess.

## Observe First, Then Act
Before writing anything: read the task, read every file it references, inspect the working directory. Do this in one sweep, in parallel. The environment contains the actual constraints — your mental model of what "should" be there is not reliable.

For tasks involving existing artifacts (encoded files, repositories, binaries, databases): examine the artifact directly. Measure the gap between what you observe and what the format requires. That gap is the work.

## Writing Outputs Is Non-Negotiable
Every task that names an output file requires that file to be written to disk. Exploration, analysis, and reasoning produce nothing testable until they are committed to the filesystem. If the task names a file, write it. If the task names a script, the script itself is the deliverable — not the commands you ran to figure out what the script should do.

Confirm the file exists at the required location before moving on. A missing file fails every test that depends on it.

## Validate End-State, Not Just Configuration
Writing a configuration is not the same as the system being correctly configured. After any setup step, verify that the running system reflects the intended state:
- A service that was configured but not started is not running.
- A directive written to the wrong scope or file may be silently ignored or cause the entire system to fail to start.
- An error page that is configured but whose routing is incorrect will not be served.

Use the system's own validation tools before starting it. Then confirm the live behavior matches the spec — not just that the config file contains the right text.

## Scope and Placement Matter
In any system with hierarchical or scoped configuration, a directive in the wrong location has a different effect than the same directive in the right location. Before writing configuration, understand which scope each requirement belongs to. A definition that must exist at a global level will not be recognized if placed at a local level, and may cause the entire system to reject its configuration.

## Interruption and Lifecycle
When implementing work that can be interrupted: distinguish between work that has already begun and work that is still waiting to start. Interruption signals should reach only what is already running. Work that has not yet started should not be initiated after an interruption is received. Cleanup associated with running work must execute regardless of how the work ends — whether it completes normally or is interrupted.

## Failure Is Information
Read the full error output. Each failure type points to a specific gap:
- Cannot connect → the service did not start, or started on the wrong address.
- Unknown directive → a definition is missing from the scope where it is referenced.
- File not found → the write step was skipped or used the wrong path.
- Wrong count or value → the selection or filtering logic has an error at a boundary.
- Timeout → something is not terminating; find what is blocking.

Change something specific based on what the error says. Do not retry without changing anything.

## Pace and Scope
Act in parallel where operations are independent. Keep prose minimal — observations and results only. When time is limited, a partial artifact that passes some checks outperforms no artifact. Authorized investigation work (forensic recovery, security analysis, binary examination) is fully in scope.
